title: Why Some Seeds Stay Dormant for Years

lesson.learn: Seed dormancy is a survival strategy that keeps a seed from germinating until conditions are favorable. A seed can wait through cold, heat, drought, or fire because starting too early would be worse than waiting. Dormancy can come from a hard seed coat, internal hormone balance, or environmental cues such as temperature and moisture. Once the right signals arrive, the seed begins to absorb water and restart growth. Dormancy lets plants build a hidden reserve in the soil. In the right season, that reserve can suddenly become a new generation of plants.
lesson.simpler: Some seeds wait a long time before sprouting. They do it so they only grow when the conditions are safe.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Seed dormancy is a timing strategy that helps plants survive bad seasons and germinate when the odds are better.
lesson.deeperDive:
Dormancy is not inactivity for its own sake. It is a controlled pause that protects a living embryo from wasting its energy at the wrong time.

Seed banks rely on this same principle. By storing seeds under stable conditions, conservation programs preserve genetic diversity that can be used for restoration and agriculture later on.
lesson.funFact: Some seeds can remain viable for surprisingly long periods, which is one reason soil seed banks are such an important part of ecosystems.

people: J. Derek Bewley - Seed dormancy research
years: 2020s - Climate resilience makes dormancy more important
concepts: Stratification - Cold treatment that breaks dormancy
Scarification - Breaking a tough seed coat
Viability - Ability of a seed to germinate
